summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a framework for non-invasive speech decoding using a CLIP-based contrastive learning objective to align EEG representations with pre-trained audio embeddings. Unlike prior work limited to small vocabularies or short recordings, this study utilizes 175 hours of EEG data from one participant to achieve 48.5% top-1 accuracy in a 512-phrase classification task. The authors provide evidence of a scaling effect where performance drops drastically when data is reduced to typical levels (~10 hours), and they validate that the model relies on neural signals rather than EMG artifacts.
key_points: Method for EEG speech decoding: Uses a CLIP-based alignment between a Conformer-based EEG encoder and pre-trained audio encoders such as wav2vec2.0.
Evidence of scaling laws: Shows a dramatic increase in zero-shot classification accuracy as training data scales from 10 to 175 hours.
Robustness to artifacts: Employs adaptive filtering and EMG-mixing data augmentation to ensure decoding is driven by neural activity rather than muscle movements.
limitations: Data collected from only a single participant, leaving cross-subject transferability unproven.
Study used healthy individuals performing overt speech, not patients with speech impairments.
Reconstructed speech waveforms lack the clarity needed for practical neuroprostheses.
Data was collected in a static seated position, limiting applicability to dynamic real-world movement.

method.architecture: EEG encoder combining HTNet and Conformer; pre-trained audio encoders (wav2vec2.0, Whisper, Encodec); 1D U-Net diffusion vocoder for reconstruction.
method.finetuning: Zero-shot classification and speech reconstruction using a diffusion-based vocoder.
method.objective: CLIP contrastive loss to align EEG and audio latent representations.
method.pretraining: Contrastive alignment of EEG and audio embeddings on 175 hours of data.

summary.one_liner: Investigates the scaling law of EEG-based open-vocabulary speech decoding using a large-scale 175-hour dataset from a single subject.
summary.title: Scaling Law in Neural Data: Non-Invasive Speech Decoding with 175 Hours of EEG Data
summary.unique_contribution: Demonstrates that increasing EEG training data to 175 hours significantly improves open-vocabulary speech decoding accuracy, revealing a clear scaling law for non-invasive neural data.

papers.title: Scaling Law in Neural Data: Non-Invasive Speech Decoding with 175 Hours of EEG Data

reasons: Explicitly investigates scaling laws for EEG representations, a core component of foundation model research
Employs self-supervised representation learning to develop latent EEG structures
Conducts a systematic scaling analysis on the relationship between data volume and decoding performance
